question_frequency.rationale: Only one didactic question is posed (the A/B choice on translate vs dict loop). Other potential teachable moments — validation strategy, error message design, whether to make the function return type explicit, handling lowercase — pass without engagement.

question_quality.rationale: The single choice is substantive and idiomatic (str.translate vs explicit loop), exposing a real Python idiom and a meaningful trade-off in error reporting. However, it doesn't probe the crux of validation design or edge cases, leaving the most subtle decisions unexplored.

scaffolding.rationale: Single question with no follow-up means there is no real progression. The post-choice exposition (walrus, maketrans timing, set difference) is decent reinforcement but doesn't build on additional questions.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm, concise, and conversational; the pair acknowledges the user's reasoning ("Exactly right") and offers natural next steps (run tests, move on). No friction or non sequiturs, though the back-and-forth is shallow.

effectiveness.rationale: Final solution is functionally equivalent to the baseline — same str.translate approach, same validation via set difference. Slightly less polished error message (raw set repr vs sorted list), but passes all 6 canonical tests.

friction.rationale: 77s vs 42s baseline (~1.8x), with 3 turns vs 1. The extra time bought one genuine didactic exchange plus actually running the canonical test suite — modest pedagogical payoff proportional to the overhead.
